1:
On Genesis 47:1 — “Then Joseph came and told Pharaoh, My father and my brethren… are come out of the land of Canaan; and, behold, they are in the land of Goshen” — the witnesses mark Joseph’s respectful report to the king.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the protocol. Adam Clarke summarizes: Joseph informs Pharaoh his father and brethren are arrived in Goshen. Ellicott: though Joseph had wished Goshen for them, Pharaoh’s permission was needed; he mentions only that they had halted there. The Puritan Henry: the respect Joseph as a subject showed his prince, not settling them till he had given notice. The Reformed Calvin: he indirectly intimates his desire for a habitation in Goshen.

The verse opens the presentation. Joseph reports his family’s arrival to Pharaoh. The favourite minister defers to his king’s authority.

So the witnesses see Joseph, for all his high office, observe a careful deference toward Pharaoh in settling his family. The witnesses note the protocol Henry admires: though Joseph was the favourite and prime minister, and though Pharaoh had himself invited the family down, Joseph would not settle them until he had given the king notice and obtained his leave; the great man does not presume on his power. They mark the substance of his report: “my father and my brethren… are come out of the land of Canaan; and, behold, they are in the land of Goshen” — he announces their arrival and that they have halted in Goshen, the region he had all along wished for them. They observe Ellicott’s point that, much as Joseph desired Goshen to be their dwelling, the grant of it lay with Pharaoh, and so he mentions only that they had stopped there, leaving the formal request to follow. They note Calvin’s sense that Joseph indirectly intimates his wish for Goshen, yet with a modesty free from cunning; he states the case plainly and trusts the king’s favour. They observe that Joseph’s conduct models the union of high station with humble submission to lawful authority. They note that he commits the matter to Pharaoh rather than acting on his own. The verse thus opens the presentation: Joseph reports his family’s arrival in Goshen — preparing to bring his brethren, in the next verse, before the king.
2:
On Genesis 47:2 — “And he took some of his brethren, even five men, and presented them unto Pharaoh” — the witnesses mark the five brothers brought before the king.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the phrase. Adam Clarke: the original, “from the extremity of his brethren he took five men,” has been understood several ways. Keil and Delitzsch trace the presentation. Ellicott: the number “five” recurs in the narrative (Genesis 43:34; Genesis 45:22), perhaps of some importance among the Egyptians. The Reformed Gill notes the various senses of “some.”

The verse presents the five. Joseph brings five of his brethren before Pharaoh. A representative number is introduced to the king.

So the witnesses see Joseph select five of his brethren to present formally to Pharaoh. The witnesses note the curious Hebrew phrase the expositors weigh: “from the extremity of his brethren he took five men” — Clarke records that it has been understood in several ways, some taking it of the meanest or least imposing of the brothers (so that, as Jewish tradition supposed, Pharaoh might not be tempted to enlist the most capable as soldiers), others of a selection by some other principle. They mark that, whatever the precise force of the idiom, Joseph chose five to stand before the king as representatives of the family. They observe Ellicott’s note on the recurring number five in the Joseph narrative — the fivefold portion at the feast, the five changes of raiment to Benjamin — suggesting the number may have carried some special significance among the Egyptians, as seven did among the Hebrews. They note that the presentation of a chosen few, rather than all eleven, suited the formality of a royal audience. They observe that Joseph, having prepared his brethren beforehand how to answer (Genesis 46:34), now brings them in to make their request through the king’s own questioning. They note that the scene is arranged with care, that the family’s settlement might be granted by Pharaoh himself. The verse thus stages the audience: five brethren presented to the king — whom Pharaoh, in the next verse, questions about their occupation.
3:
On Genesis 47:3 — “And Pharaoh said unto his brethren, What is your occupation? And they said… Thy servants are shepherds, both we, and also our fathers” — the witnesses mark the question and the confession of their calling.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: Joseph had instructed them to add “also our fathers” (Genesis 46:34), since occupations were hereditary among the Egyptians, so Pharaoh would see no change was possible. Wesley: Pharaoh takes it for granted they had something to do; all should have an employment. The Reformed Calvin: this confession was humiliating, for among the Egyptians the shepherd’s life was held infamous.

The verse gives the question and answer. Pharaoh asks their occupation; they own that they and their fathers are shepherds. They confess a calling the Egyptians despised.

So the witnesses see the brothers answer Pharaoh’s question with the plain confession of their humble calling. The witnesses note the king’s inquiry: “What is your occupation?” — the very question Joseph had foretold and prepared them to meet; Wesley observes that Pharaoh takes for granted they had some employment, and draws the lesson that all who have a place in the world should have an occupation in it. They mark their reply: “Thy servants are shepherds, both we, and also our fathers” — they own their trade frankly, and, as Ellicott notes, add the words about their fathers by Joseph’s instruction, since callings were hereditary in Egypt; this would show Pharaoh that no change of life was to be expected of them, and so dispose him to grant them the separate pasture-land of Goshen. They observe Calvin’s point that this confession was humiliating, especially to Joseph in his high dignity, for among the Egyptians the keeping of sheep was reckoned a base and even infamous calling; yet the brothers do not hide or disguise their condition. They note the providence in this very lowliness: the Egyptian contempt for shepherds, which kept the Hebrews apart in Goshen, preserved the holy seed from mingling with the people of the land. They observe that honesty about their humble occupation, far from harming them, secures their separate dwelling. The verse thus records the confession: they are shepherds, as their fathers were — the ground on which, in the next verse, they ask to dwell in Goshen.
4:
On Genesis 47:4 — “For to sojourn in the land are we come; for thy servants have no pasture for their flocks… now therefore… let thy servants dwell in the land of Goshen” — the witnesses mark the request to sojourn in Goshen.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: they ask only a temporary stay; despite the famine there was pasture in Goshen, watered by the Nile, where they could keep their flocks alive. The Reformed Gill: they come to sojourn, not to obtain inheritances, keeping in mind that Canaan was given to them. Wesley: not to settle for ever, but while the famine prevailed.

The verse states their petition. They come to sojourn, the famine being sore, and ask to dwell in Goshen. They seek a temporary refuge, not a home.

So the witnesses see the brothers make their petition to Pharaoh, asking leave to sojourn in Goshen. The witnesses note the careful word: “to sojourn in the land are we come” — they ask, as the expositors stress, only for a temporary stay, not for permanent possession or inheritance; Gill observes that they kept in mind that Canaan, not Egypt, was the land promised to them, and so sought Egypt only as a passing refuge. They mark the reason given: “thy servants have no pasture for their flocks; for the famine is sore in the land of Canaan” — the dearth had left their cattle without grazing in Canaan, which lay high and was scorched, while Goshen, watered by the Nile’s overflow, still afforded pasture; the famine drove them to seek grass for their flocks. They observe the petition: “let thy servants dwell in the land of Goshen” — they name the very region Joseph had desired for them, a pasture-land on Egypt’s border, fit for shepherds and set apart from the Egyptians. They note Wesley’s point that they asked not to settle for ever but only while the famine prevailed. They observe that the request, framed as a sojourn, both secured their immediate need and kept their hope fixed on Canaan. They note the providence that lodged them apart in Goshen. The verse thus records their petition: leave to sojourn in Goshen during the famine — which Pharaoh, through Joseph, grants in the verses following.
5:
On Genesis 47:5 — “And Pharaoh spake unto Joseph, saying, Thy father and thy brethren are come unto thee” — the witnesses mark Pharaoh’s gracious turning to Joseph.

The Reformed commentators note the king’s favour. Gill: Joseph was present at the conversation. Calvin: it is to be ascribed to God’s favour that Pharaoh was not offended that they desired a separate dwelling, for kings bear nothing more indignantly than that their favours should be rejected.

The verse turns to Joseph. Pharaoh addresses Joseph about his arrived family. The king commits the settlement to his minister.

So the witnesses see Pharaoh, hearing the brothers’ request, turn graciously to Joseph to arrange the matter. The witnesses note that Joseph stood present throughout the audience, as Gill observes, hearing all that passed between the king and his brethren. They mark that Pharaoh now speaks not to the brothers but to Joseph: “thy father and thy brethren are come unto thee” — the king acknowledges the arrival of Joseph’s family and commits to his trusted minister the disposing of them. They observe Calvin’s discerning point on the providence at work in Pharaoh’s response: it is to be ascribed to the special favour of God that the king took no offence at the brothers’ desire to dwell apart in Goshen, for there is scarcely anything that kings bear more indignantly than to have their favours, as it were, refused or restricted; yet Pharaoh, far from being displeased that they sought a separate, humble pasture rather than a share in the wealth of Egypt, freely grants their wish. They note that God so inclined the king’s heart that the very separateness the Hebrews needed — to keep them distinct from the Egyptians — was readily allowed. They observe that the favour shown to Joseph overflows to his whole house. They note that the king’s graciousness is the fruit of God’s ordering. The verse thus turns the matter to Joseph: Pharaoh bids him receive and settle his family — with the generous grant the next verse records.

So the witnesses see Pharaoh, hearing the brothers’ request, turn graciously to Joseph to arrange the matter. They note that Joseph stood present throughout the audience, hearing all that passed (Gill). They mark that Pharaoh now speaks not to the brothers but to Joseph: “thy father and thy brethren are come unto thee” — committing to his trusted minister the disposing of them. They observe Calvin’s point on the providence at work: it is to be ascribed to the special favour of God that the king took no offence at their desire to dwell apart in Goshen, for kings bear nothing more indignantly than to have their favours restricted; yet Pharaoh freely grants their wish. They note that God so inclined the king’s heart that the very separateness the Hebrews needed — to keep them distinct from the Egyptians — was readily allowed; the favour to Joseph overflows to his whole house.
6:
On Genesis 47:6 — “The land of Egypt is before thee; in the best of the land make thy father and brethren to dwell; in the land of Goshen… and if thou knowest any men of activity among them, then make them rulers over my cattle” — the witnesses mark Pharaoh’s generous grant.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: so Goshen was the best of the land; “men of activity” (anshey chayil), stout, robust men, capable of the charge. The Reformed Calvin: nothing was given Jacob but at the king’s command; the greater Joseph’s power, the more strictly he was bound not to be liberal at the king’s expense.

The verse records the grant. Pharaoh gives them the best of the land, Goshen, and offers office to the capable. The king’s bounty exceeds the request.

So the witnesses see Pharaoh grant the family more than they had asked, in royal generosity. The witnesses note the open offer: “the land of Egypt is before thee” — the king places the whole land at Joseph’s disposal for the choice of his family’s dwelling. They mark the specific grant: “in the best of the land make thy father and brethren to dwell; in the land of Goshen” — Pharaoh assigns them the very region they desired, and the expositors note that Goshen was thereby reckoned the best of the land, at least for pasture and for their needs; Clarke draws from this that Goshen was the choicest part of Egypt. They observe the further bounty: “if thou knowest any men of activity among them, then make them rulers over my cattle” — the king offers to entrust the most capable of the brothers with the oversight of the royal herds; “men of activity,” as Clarke renders it, are robust, able men fit for such charge. They note Calvin’s careful observation that all this was given at the king’s own command, not by Joseph’s private disposal; the higher Joseph’s power, the more scrupulous he was not to be generous with the king’s goods without warrant. They observe that Pharaoh’s liberality, exceeding the request, is the fruit of his regard for Joseph and of God’s overruling favour. They note the dignity offered the once-despised shepherds. The verse thus records the grant: the best of the land and office for the able — the settlement Joseph carries out, after presenting his father, in the verses to come.
7:
On Genesis 47:7 — “And Joseph brought in Jacob his father, and set him before Pharaoh: and Jacob blessed Pharaoh” — the witnesses mark the patriarch’s blessing of the king.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Jacob saluted him with “Peace be unto thee” or the like. Ellicott: the presentation of Jacob was a more solemn matter than that of the brethren. The Puritan Henry: with the gravity of old age, the piety of a believer, and the authority of a patriarch and prophet, Jacob besought the Lord to bless Pharaoh. The Reformed Calvin: some time had intervened before Jacob was brought.

The verse records the meeting. Joseph presents Jacob, who blesses Pharaoh. The aged patriarch confers a blessing on the king.

So the witnesses see the aged Jacob brought before Pharaoh, and the patriarch bless the king. The witnesses note the solemnity Ellicott marks: the presentation of Jacob was a weightier matter than that of the brethren — Pharaoh, who had looked on the brothers with interest as kin to his vizier, now receives the venerable father himself. They mark the act of blessing: “Jacob blessed Pharaoh” — the old man, on entering the royal presence, pronounces a blessing on the king. They observe the range of what this signified, as Clarke and Henry draw it out: in part a courteous salutation, a “peace be unto thee” and grateful good-will to the benefactor of his house; but more than mere civility, an act of piety — Jacob, with the gravity of age, the faith of a believer, and the authority of a patriarch and prophet, besought God to bestow a blessing on Pharaoh. They note the dignity of the scene the expositors find here: a poor, aged shepherd, a sojourner driven by famine, yet blesses the mighty king of Egypt — the lesser in worldly estate conferring the greater spiritual gift; for it is the patriarch, heir of the promises, who blesses, and the monarch who receives. They observe Calvin’s note that some time had passed before Jacob was thus brought, the family being first settled. They observe that Jacob is not ashamed of his religion but expresses it in blessing his host. The verse thus records the meeting: Jacob blesses Pharaoh — before the king, in the next verse, asks his age.
8:
On Genesis 47:8 — “And Pharaoh said unto Jacob, How old art thou?” — the witnesses mark the king’s question about Jacob’s age.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the wonder it implies. Gill: Dr. Lightfoot thinks Pharaoh had never seen so old a man; the phrase, “how many are the days of the years of thy life?” Jacob takes up in his answer. Keil and Delitzsch and Wesley: a question put to old men, for it is natural to admire and reverence age; in Egypt people were not so long-lived.

The verse gives the question. Pharaoh asks Jacob how old he is. The patriarch’s great age strikes the king.

So the witnesses see Pharaoh, struck by Jacob’s venerable appearance, ask his age. The witnesses note the form of the question: “How old art thou?” — or, as the Hebrew runs, “how many are the days of the years of thy life?” — a phrasing, Gill observes, that Jacob takes up and turns to good account in his answer. They mark the wonder behind the question, which the expositors discern: the king, as Lightfoot supposed, had perhaps never seen so aged a man, for in Egypt, where life was shorter, such great age was rare; Jacob’s countenance, worn by years of labour and sorrow, plainly bespoke an extraordinary span. They observe Wesley’s lesson that this is the question naturally put to the old, for it is native to us to admire and reverence old age; the king honours the patriarch’s years. They note that the question opens the way for Jacob’s memorable reply, in which he will reckon his life a pilgrimage, few and evil. They observe that even the mighty Pharaoh is moved to wonder and respect before the aged saint; worldly greatness defers to the dignity of years and of faith. They note that the inquiry is courteous and admiring, not idle. The verse thus records the king’s question: how old is Jacob? — drawing forth, in the next verse, the patriarch’s confession of his pilgrim life.

So the witnesses see Pharaoh, struck by Jacob’s venerable appearance, ask his age. They note the form of the question: “How old art thou?” — or, as the Hebrew runs, “how many are the days of the years of thy life?” — a phrasing, Gill observes, that Jacob takes up in his answer. They mark the wonder behind it: the king, as Lightfoot supposed, had perhaps never seen so aged a man, for in Egypt, where life was shorter, such great age was rare; Jacob’s countenance, worn by labour and sorrow, bespoke an extraordinary span. They observe Wesley’s lesson that this is the question naturally put to the old, for it is native to us to admire and reverence old age. They note that the question opens the way for Jacob’s memorable reply, in which he reckons his life a pilgrimage, few and evil; even mighty Pharaoh is moved to wonder and respect before the aged saint.
9:
On Genesis 47:9 — “The days of the years of my pilgrimage are an hundred and thirty years: few and evil have the days of the years of my life been, and have not attained unto the days of the years of the life of my fathers” — the witnesses mark Jacob’s confession of a pilgrim life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Adam Clarke: “pilgrimage” (megurai), sojourning, wandering; Jacob lived a migratory life. Ellicott: literally “my sojournings,” a stranger settled in a land not his own (cf. 1 Peter 2:11). Wesley: he looked on himself as a stranger in this world, a traveller toward another. The Reformed Calvin weighs why he names his days few and evil rather than recounting God’s favours.

The verse gives Jacob’s answer. His years are a pilgrimage, few and evil, short of his fathers’. He owns himself a stranger on the earth.

So the witnesses see Jacob answer the king with a confession that lifts the moment above mere age. The witnesses note the great word: he calls his life “my pilgrimage” — or, as the expositors render it, his “sojournings,” his wandering; he had lived a migratory life in Canaan, Mesopotamia, and now Egypt, scarcely ever at rest. They mark the deeper sense the witnesses draw out: Jacob looked on himself not merely as a wanderer in this present journey but as a stranger and pilgrim in the world itself, a traveller toward a better, heavenly country; Wesley and Gill find here the believer’s confession that he is not at home on earth but seeks a country to come, the very faith the New Testament names in the patriarchs (1 Peter 2:11). They observe the tally: “an hundred and thirty years” — great by Egyptian reckoning, yet, says Jacob, “few and evil,” short and full of trouble, and not reaching the span of Abraham and Isaac. They note Calvin’s reflection on the seeming complaint: Jacob does not murmur, but, mindful of his many afflictions, owns the brevity and hardship of his earthly course; yet his calling it a “pilgrimage” shows that his hope rested beyond it. They observe that the aged saint, before the king of Egypt, confesses himself a stranger looking for home. The verse thus records the confession: his years a pilgrimage, few and evil — after which, in the next verse, he blesses Pharaoh and departs.
10:
On Genesis 47:10 — “And Jacob blessed Pharaoh, and went out from before Pharaoh” — the witnesses mark the parting blessing.

The Reformed Gill: as when he came in, so when he took his leave, he blessed Pharaoh, wishing him all happiness and thanking him for the honour shown. Wesley: not only an act of civility but of piety; he prayed for him, as one having the authority of a prophet and patriarch — a blessing not to be despised even by a potent prince.

The verse closes the audience. Jacob blesses Pharaoh again and withdraws. The patriarch leaves a blessing as he goes.

So the witnesses see Jacob take his leave of the king with a second blessing, framing the whole interview in benediction. The witnesses note the repetition: as Jacob had blessed Pharaoh on entering (v. 7), so now, departing, “he blessed Pharaoh, and went out from before Pharaoh” — the audience begins and ends with the patriarch’s blessing. They mark Gill’s sense that this parting blessing was a wishing of all happiness to the king and a grateful acknowledgment of the honour and kindness shown to his house. They observe Wesley’s point that the blessing was more than mere courtesy: it was an act of piety, Jacob praying for Pharaoh as one bearing the authority of a prophet and a patriarch; and such a blessing, from such a man, was not a thing to be despised even by so mighty a prince. They note the dignity the expositors find in it: the heir of the covenant, though a poor sojourner, stands in a place to bless the king of Egypt, the lesser in the world’s account conferring the greater gift. They observe that Jacob, grateful and reverent, does not leave the royal presence without commending Pharaoh to God. They note that the believer’s parting word is a blessing. The verse thus closes the audience: Jacob blesses Pharaoh and withdraws — the family now to be settled, as the next verses record, in the best of the land.

So the witnesses see Jacob take his leave with a second blessing, framing the whole interview in benediction. They note the repetition: as he had blessed Pharaoh on entering (v7), so now, departing, “he blessed Pharaoh, and went out” — the audience begins and ends with the patriarch’s blessing. They mark Gill’s sense that this parting blessing wished all happiness to the king and acknowledged the honour shown his house. They observe Wesley’s point that it was more than courtesy: an act of piety, Jacob praying for Pharaoh as one bearing the authority of a prophet and patriarch; such a blessing, from such a man, was not to be despised even by so mighty a prince. They note the dignity the expositors find in it: the heir of the covenant, though a poor sojourner, stands in a place to bless the king of Egypt, the lesser in the world’s account conferring the greater gift. The believer’s parting word is a blessing.
11:
On Genesis 47:11 — “And Joseph placed his father and his brethren, and gave them a possession in the land of Egypt, in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded” — the witnesses mark the settlement in the best of the land.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Joseph assigned his father and brethren a possession in the best of the land, the land of Rameses, according to Pharaoh’s command. The Reformed Gill: houses to dwell in, lands to till, pastures for their flocks and herds.

The verse records the settling. Joseph gives them a possession in Rameses, the best of the land, by Pharaoh’s command. The family is established in Goshen’s choicest part.

So the witnesses see Joseph carry out the king’s grant, settling his family in the best of the land. The witnesses note the act: “Joseph placed his father and his brethren, and gave them a possession in the land of Egypt” — he provides them a fixed dwelling and holding, as Gill draws out, with houses to live in, land to till, and pasture for their flocks and herds. They mark the place: “in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses” — understood as the choicest district of Goshen, the region named (here or later) for one of its cities; the fertile border-land suited to shepherds. They observe the warrant repeated: “as Pharaoh had commanded” — Joseph acts strictly by the king’s order, settling them not on his own authority but on Pharaoh’s express grant; the scrupulousness the expositors noted earlier holds here too. They note the providence in the placement: the family is established in a rich and separate region, where they may grow and multiply apart from the Egyptians, secure through the famine. They observe that the promise to bring Jacob’s house down and preserve them is being fulfilled through Joseph’s care and Pharaoh’s bounty. They note that the once-imperilled family is now settled in plenty. The verse thus records the settlement: a possession in the best of the land, by Pharaoh’s command — where Joseph, the next verse adds, nourishes them with bread.
12:
On Genesis 47:12 — “And Joseph nourished his father, and his brethren, and all his father’s household, with bread, according to their families” — the witnesses mark Joseph’s provision for his kin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the phrase. Keil and Delitzsch trace it; Ellicott: “according to their families” (Heb. taph) means according to the number of dependants each had, so that the smallest household was not starved. Calvin notes the Hebrew idiom, “to the mouth of the little one,” some reading it of feeding them as children are fed.

The verse records his care. Joseph nourishes the whole household with bread, by their numbers. He provides amply for every family.

So the witnesses see Joseph, beyond settling his family, undertake their daily nourishment through the famine. The witnesses note the provision: “Joseph nourished his father, and his brethren, and all his father’s household, with bread” — though Goshen afforded pasture for their cattle, it could not feed their families with corn while the famine continued; so Joseph supplied bread for them all, as Gill observes, sustaining the whole company. They mark the careful measure: “according to their families” — the expositors explain the Hebrew (taph) as meaning according to the number of children and dependants in each household, so that the provision was apportioned to need; Ellicott notes that thus even the brother with but one child, and the brother with many, were each supplied, and none starved for want of a fair allowance. They observe Calvin’s attention to the idiom, “to the mouth of the little one,” which some read as feeding them tenderly, as food is conveyed to the mouths of children. They note the generosity and faithfulness of Joseph: with the whole grain of Egypt in his charge, he sees to it that his own kin lack nothing, providing for each according to their families. They observe the type the chapter quietly holds — the brother once sold now the sustainer of those who sold him. They note that the family is kept alive by Joseph’s hand. The verse thus records his provision: he nourished the whole household with bread, by their numbers — while the wider land, the next verses show, is brought to extremity.
13:
On Genesis 47:13 — “And there was no bread in all the land; for the famine was very sore, so that the land of Egypt and all the land of Canaan fainted by reason of the famine” — the witnesses mark the severity of the famine.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: “fainted” (tilah, to languish, be exhausted). The Puritan Henry: an account of the saving of Egypt from ruin, for God is King of nations as well as of saints. The Reformed Calvin: a memorable judgment, that the most fertile regions were reduced to such poverty.

The verse states the extremity. The famine was so sore that Egypt and Canaan fainted for want of bread. The land is brought to the edge of ruin.

So the witnesses see the narrative turn from Joseph’s family to the dire state of the whole land under the famine. The witnesses note the extremity: “there was no bread in all the land” — all private stores were exhausted, and bread was to be had only from Pharaoh’s granaries, which Joseph had filled in the years of plenty. They mark the severity: “the famine was very sore, so that the land of Egypt and all the land of Canaan fainted” — the word, the expositors note, means to languish, to be utterly exhausted; both countries were drained and worn down by hunger. They observe Calvin’s reflection on the wonder of it: a memorable judgment of God, that the most fertile regions, accustomed to supply grain even to distant nations, were themselves reduced to such poverty as nearly to be consumed; the richest lands made destitute. They note Henry’s observation that the chapter now records God’s saving of Egypt from ruin, for the same providence that preserved Jacob’s family reaches to the nations; God is King of nations as well as King of saints, ordering the affairs of kingdoms. They observe that the depth of the dearth sets the stage for Joseph’s great administration of relief, by which a perishing people is kept alive. They note the contrast with Goshen, where Joseph’s kin were fed. The verse thus states the crisis: Egypt and Canaan faint under the sore famine — the distress Joseph meets, in the next verses, by gathering the people’s money for corn.
14:
On Genesis 47:14 — “And Joseph gathered up all the money that was found in the land of Egypt, and in the land of Canaan, for the corn which they bought: and Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh’s house” — the witnesses mark the gathering of the money.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: by selling corn out of the public stores till the money failed, all the money was exchanged for corn and brought into Pharaoh’s treasury. Keil and Delitzsch trace it. The Reformed Calvin: Moses commends the sincere and faithful administration of Joseph, who brought all into the king’s house, keeping nothing for himself.

The verse records the first stage of relief. Joseph sells corn until all the money of Egypt and Canaan is gathered to Pharaoh. He administers the stores with strict fidelity.

So the witnesses see Joseph begin his great administration of the famine relief, gathering the people’s money in exchange for grain. The witnesses note the transaction: “Joseph gathered up all the money… for the corn which they bought” — not by any exaction, but by selling corn from the royal stores to the starving people of both Egypt and Canaan, until, as Clarke explains, all the money in both lands had been paid in for grain. They mark the destination of it: “Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh’s house” — the whole proceeds went into the king’s treasury. They observe Calvin’s emphasis on the fidelity this shows: Moses commends the sincere and faithful administration of Joseph, who, having sole charge of the corn and handling vast sums, kept nothing back for himself but brought all into Pharaoh’s house; few men so entrusted could resist enriching themselves, yet Joseph was incorruptibly honest. They note that the gathering of the money is the first stage of a policy by which the perishing are fed and the crown enriched together; the people receive life, Pharaoh receives their silver. They observe that Joseph’s diligence and integrity together preserve the nation. They note that the relief is conducted with order and justice, not plunder. The verse thus records the first stage: all the money gathered for corn into Pharaoh’s house — until, the next verse shows, the money fails.
15:
On Genesis 47:15 — “And when money failed… all the Egyptians came unto Joseph, and said, Give us bread: for why should we die in thy presence? for the money faileth” — the witnesses mark the failing of the money and the people’s cry.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: when the money was exhausted the Egyptians came demanding bread. The Reformed Gill: it had been spent in the third, fourth, and fifth years. Calvin: not that all the money in Egypt was in the treasury, but nearly all was exhausted, so that the common people had none.

The verse records the money’s end. The money failing, the people cry to Joseph for bread. Want drives them to the next desperate stage.

So the witnesses see the relief reach its first crisis: the people’s money is spent, and they cry for bread. The witnesses note the failing: “when money failed in the land of Egypt, and in the land of Canaan” — after years of buying corn, the silver of both lands was at last exhausted; Gill reckons it spent through the earlier years of the famine, so that by the sixth year the people had no more. They mark Calvin’s qualification that this does not mean every coin had passed into the royal treasury — some of the nobles still had means — but that nearly all was gone, and the common people in particular were left destitute of money. They observe the desperate appeal: “all the Egyptians came unto Joseph, and said, Give us bread” — the starving people throng to him who alone has corn, begging to be fed. They note the pathos of their plea: “why should we die in thy presence? for the money faileth” — they will perish before his very eyes unless he gives them bread, and they have nothing left to pay; they cast themselves on his mercy. They observe that the cry, “why should we die?” presses Joseph for a new means of relief now that money is gone. They note that the famine drives the people from one extremity to the next — first their money, then their cattle, then their land and persons. The verse thus records the crisis: the money failing, the people cry for bread — to which Joseph, in the next verse, answers by taking their cattle.
16:
On Genesis 47:16 — “And Joseph said, Give your cattle; and I will give you for your cattle, if money fail” — the witnesses mark the exchange of cattle for bread.

The grammatical-historical commentators commend the measure. Adam Clarke: the wisest measure, for the preservation of both people and cattle, since the people had no grain to feed their beasts. Ellicott: a merciful arrangement, the owners delivered from a burden and a portion of the cattle saved. The Reformed Calvin: Joseph might seem cruel, but gives bread for cattle out of necessity.

The verse gives Joseph’s second provision. Their money gone, he takes their cattle for bread. The exchange relieves both man and beast.

So the witnesses see Joseph meet the people’s second extremity by taking their cattle in exchange for bread. The witnesses note the proposal: “Give your cattle; and I will give you for your cattle, if money fail” — since they have no more silver, Joseph accepts their livestock as the price of grain. They mark the wisdom and mercy of the measure, which the expositors are at pains to commend. Clarke calls it the wisest possible step for the preservation of both people and cattle: the people had no grain even to feed their beasts, so that the animals must otherwise have starved; by surrendering them for bread, the owners were fed and the cattle, gathered into Pharaoh’s keeping, were kept alive. Ellicott likewise finds it a merciful arrangement, by which the owners were relieved of a burden they could no longer sustain and a portion of the cattle was saved. They observe Calvin’s defence: Joseph might seem cruel in not giving bread freely, yet he acts under the constraint of a public stewardship, dispensing the stores justly so that all may be carried through the famine. They note that the exchange is neither extortion nor charity but a prudent administration that preserves the nation through a second year of want. They observe that the cattle, like the money before, pass to Pharaoh, while the people gain another year of life. The verse thus records the second provision: cattle given for bread — sustaining the people, as the next verse shows, for that year.
17:
On Genesis 47:17 — “And they brought their cattle unto Joseph: and Joseph gave them bread in exchange for horses, and for the flocks… and he fed them with bread for all their cattle for that year” — the witnesses mark the year sustained by cattle.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: the mention of horses is an important fact in dating the dynasty, for horses came late to Egypt; he fed them for all their cattle for that year. The Reformed Gill: they brought their cattle readily, there being scarce any grass to feed them.

The verse records the exchange carried out. The people bring their cattle, and Joseph feeds them for a year. Livestock buys them another season of bread.

So the witnesses see the people accept Joseph’s terms and bring in their cattle for bread. The witnesses note the readiness with which they came: “they brought their cattle unto Joseph” — the more willingly, Gill observes, since there was scarcely any grass left to feed the beasts, so that to surrender them for grain was no great loss but a relief. They mark the full list of what was exchanged: “horses, and… flocks… herds… asses” — every kind of livestock passed to Joseph for bread. They observe Ellicott’s historical note on the mention of horses: that horses appear here is a significant fact for dating the period, since the horse was a comparatively late arrival in Egypt, not known in Abraham’s time; its presence points to the dynasty under which Joseph served. They note the result: “he fed them with bread for all their cattle for that year” — the surrender of the livestock bought the people a whole further year of sustenance through the famine. They observe that Joseph’s administration thus carries the nation through a second stage of want, the cattle now in Pharaoh’s charge and the people fed. They note that the relief, though it strips the people by degrees, keeps them alive. The verse thus records the year sustained: cattle given, bread received for that year — until, the next verse shows, that year too is ended and only land and persons remain.
18:
On Genesis 47:18 — “When that year was ended, they came unto him the second year, and said… our money is spent; my lord also hath our herds of cattle; there is not ought left… but our bodies, and our lands” — the witnesses mark the last extremity.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the seventh and last year of the famine, in which the land must be sown for a future crop. Ellicott: not the second year of the famine, but the year following the giving up of the cattle. The Reformed Calvin: Moses reckons the second year from the failing of the money; knowing the dearth’s end was near, they desired seed as well as corn.

The verse records the final stage. Money and cattle gone, only their bodies and lands remain. The people are stripped to their last possession.

So the witnesses see the people, in the famine’s last year, come to Joseph having nothing left but themselves and their land. The witnesses note the time: “when that year was ended, they came unto him the second year” — not, the expositors clarify, the second year of the famine, but the year following the surrender of their cattle, which Clarke identifies as the seventh and last year of the dearth, the year in which the land must be sown if there was to be any future crop. They mark the people’s confession of their utter destitution: “our money is spent; my lord also hath our herds of cattle; there is not ought left… but our bodies, and our lands.” They observe the stripping by stages the chapter has traced: first their silver, then their livestock, and now, all else gone, only their own persons and their fields remain to offer. They note Calvin’s observation that, knowing from Joseph’s prophecy that the famine’s end was at hand, they sought not only food to live but seed to sow, that the land might yield again. They observe the desperation of men reduced to their last possession, throwing themselves wholly on Joseph’s administration. They note that the famine has brought the whole nation to the point where they will sell themselves and their land for bread and seed. They observe that this final extremity sets up the great transaction of the verses following. The verse thus records the last stage: nothing left but their bodies and their lands — which, in the next verse, they offer to Pharaoh.
19:
On Genesis 47:19 — “Buy us and our land for bread, and we and our land will be servants unto Pharaoh: and give us seed, that we may live, and not die, that the land be not desolate” — the witnesses mark the people’s offer of themselves.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the proposal: buy us and our land, and give us seed, that we live and the land be not desolate. Adam Clarke: in famines, as in India, men have sold their children, and whole families, to prevent perishing. The Reformed Gill: die they must, unless they had bread; and their land too, if they had no seed.

The verse gives their proposal. They offer to sell themselves and their land for bread and seed. Survival is bought with their freedom.

So the witnesses see the people themselves propose the surrender of their persons and lands. The witnesses note the desperate offer, made by the people, not imposed by Joseph: “buy us and our land for bread, and we and our land will be servants unto Pharaoh” — reduced to their last extremity, they freely offer to become Pharaoh’s bondservants and to give up their fields, if only they may have bread to live. They mark the further request: “give us seed, that we may live, and not die, that the land be not desolate” — they ask not only present food but seed-corn for sowing, that the land might be cultivated again and not lie waste; knowing the famine near its end, they look to a future crop. They observe Gill’s plain sense: without bread they must die, and without seed their land must die too, becoming desolate; both man and field needed Joseph’s relief. They note Clarke’s parallel from later famines, where men have sold their children and whole families to escape starvation, so that the people’s offer of themselves was, in such straits, not unheard of. They observe that the transaction by which the people become Pharaoh’s servants is begun at their own request, in the face of death. They note that they prize life and the saving of the land above their freedom and possessions. The verse thus records their proposal: buy us and our land, and give us seed — which Joseph, in the next verses, accepts.
20:
On Genesis 47:20 — “And Joseph bought all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh… so the land became Pharaoh’s” — the witnesses mark the purchase of the land, and a debated policy.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: Joseph has been accused of reducing a free people to slavery; undoubtedly he increased the royal power, yet he broke the yoke of a multitude of petty lords. The Reformed Gill: he bought it for Pharaoh, not for himself nor his posterity. Calvin defends him: it might seem the height of cruel avarice, but it was not so.

The verse records the purchase. Joseph buys all Egypt’s land for Pharaoh. The crown becomes sole proprietor — a policy variously judged.

So the witnesses see Joseph accept the people’s offer and buy the whole land of Egypt for the crown. The witnesses note the transaction: “Joseph bought all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh… so the land became Pharaoh’s” — the Egyptians, selling their fields for bread, made the king the sole owner of the soil. They mark Gill’s point that Joseph bought it for Pharaoh, not for himself nor to entail on his own family; his integrity is preserved, the gain going wholly to the crown. They observe the debate the expositors record over this policy. Some have accused Joseph of reducing a free people to slavery and concentrating all power in the king’s hands. The witnesses note the defence offered. Ellicott grants that Joseph greatly increased the royal power, yet argues he thereby broke the yoke of the many petty lords and hereditary princes under whom the people had lived, perhaps bettering rather than worsening their lot. Calvin insists that, though the measure might at first sight seem the height of cruel avarice — taking from the husbandmen the very fields by which they fed the kingdom — it was in truth no such thing, but a just and necessary administration in a public emergency, the people themselves consenting. They observe that the land passed to Pharaoh by purchase and the people’s own request, not by seizure. They note that the judgement of Joseph’s policy has divided readers. The verse thus records the purchase: all Egypt’s land made Pharaoh’s — the people, as the next verse adds, removed to the cities.
21:
On Genesis 47:21 — “And as for the people, he removed them to cities from one end of the borders of Egypt even to the other end thereof” — the witnesses mark the removal of the people.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it mercifully. Adam Clarke: Joseph was influenced by humanity and prudence, not policy; the corn being stored in the cities, the people were more easily fed near it. Ellicott: most merciful, that they might be sure of nourishment near the food. The Reformed Calvin: the removal was severe, yet better than to be slaves attached to the soil. Wesley: to show Pharaoh’s sovereign power, that they might forget their old titles.

The verse records the relocation. Joseph removes the people to cities throughout Egypt. The measure is read as merciful provision.

So the witnesses see Joseph relocate the people to the cities, and the expositors weigh his purpose. The witnesses note the act: “he removed them to cities from one end of the borders of Egypt even to the other” — the whole population was shifted into or near the cities. They mark the merciful reading several commentators give. Clarke holds that Joseph was moved not by political design but by humanity and prudence: since the corn was stored in the cities, the people could be most easily and surely fed if they were near it; Ellicott agrees, finding it a most merciful measure, that the people might be certain of nourishment close to the food. They observe a second purpose some discern: Wesley suggests the removal also displayed Pharaoh’s sovereign right over the now-purchased land, and helped the people, transplanted from their old fields, to forget their former titles and accept their new condition. They note Calvin’s balanced judgment: the removal was indeed severe, yet it was better that the people should be moved and remain free cultivators of the land than be left attached to their old soil as serfs bound to it; the change, though hard, was to their advantage. They observe that the measure, however variously read, is generally defended as wise and humane rather than cruel. They note that Joseph’s aim was the preservation of the people through the famine. The verse thus records the removal: the people shifted to the cities — a measure that, the next verses show, did not touch the priests’ land.
22:
On Genesis 47:22 — “Only the land of the priests bought he not; for the priests had a portion assigned them of Pharaoh, and did eat their portion… wherefore they sold not their lands” — the witnesses mark the exemption of the priests.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the religion of Egypt was established by law and supported by the state; Joseph bought not the priests’ lands. Ellicott: Herodotus notes the priests still had a daily allowance of cooked food. The Reformed Gill: not from any veneration of them, but because they had no need to sell, having a maintenance from Pharaoh. Calvin: they were exempted, the king granting them support.

The verse notes the exception. The priests, kept by Pharaoh’s allowance, did not sell their land. Only their estate remained untouched.

So the witnesses see one class alone exempted from the great sale: the priests of Egypt. The witnesses note the exception: “only the land of the priests bought he not” — amid the universal surrender of the land, the priests’ estate was not taken. They mark the reason the text itself gives, and which the expositors are careful to stress: “the priests had a portion assigned them of Pharaoh, and did eat their portion… wherefore they sold not their lands.” Gill insists this was not from any special affection or superstitious reverence Joseph bore the heathen priests — which could never be thought of so good a man — but simply because they had no need to sell: the king allowed them a fixed maintenance, so they were not reduced, as the rest were, to part with their fields for bread. They observe Calvin’s agreement that the priests were exempted because Pharaoh granted them support, whether a present supply or a standing provision. They note Clarke’s and Ellicott’s historical observations: that the religion of Egypt was established and maintained by the state, and that, as Herodotus records, the priests long continued to receive a daily allowance of food — a usage perhaps reaching back to this time. They observe that the exemption confirms the priests’ favoured standing in Egypt, sustained by the crown apart from the common distress. They note that Joseph’s dealing here was governed by their having no need, not by partiality. The verse thus records the exception: the priests’ land alone unbought, they being kept by Pharaoh — a detail set within the policy the next verses complete.
23:
On Genesis 47:23 — “Then Joseph said unto the people, Behold, I have bought you this day and your land for Pharaoh: lo, here is seed for you, and ye shall sow the land” — the witnesses mark the giving of seed.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: Joseph gives seed when the famine is nearly over, that they may sow; the kingdom was previously a limited monarchy. Adam Clarke: before Joseph, the king had his estates, the priests their lands, the people their patrimony. The Reformed Calvin: Joseph’s singular humanity, reinstating the destitute and exiled men.

The verse records the new arrangement. Having bought them, Joseph gives them seed to sow the land. The people are reinstated as cultivators.

So the witnesses see Joseph, the purchase complete, turn at once to restore the people as tenants of the land with seed to sow. The witnesses note his announcement: “I have bought you this day and your land for Pharaoh” — he states plainly the new state of things, that people and land alike now belong to the crown. They mark the immediate provision he adds: “lo, here is seed for you, and ye shall sow the land” — the famine drawing to its close, Joseph furnishes the seed-corn they had asked, that they may plant the fields and reap again; Ellicott notes that the timing shows the arrangement was completed near the end of the seventh year, when sowing could resume. They observe Calvin’s emphasis on the humanity of this: Joseph does not leave the people stripped and idle, but reinstates men who were utterly destitute, in a sense exiles on their own soil, setting them to work their fields again as Pharaoh’s tenants. They note the expositors’ observation that Egypt before Joseph had been a limited monarchy, the king, the priests, and the people each holding their own; now the people hold their land of Pharaoh, but they hold it still and till it. They observe that the policy, far from reducing them to idle slavery, restores them to productive life. They note that Joseph’s administration ends in the people’s preservation and renewed industry. The verse thus records the reinstatement: people bought, yet given seed to sow — on the terms the next verse sets.
24:
On Genesis 47:24 — “And it shall come to pass in the increase, that ye shall give the fifth part unto Pharaoh, and four parts shall be your own, for seed… and for your food… and for your little ones” — the witnesses mark the moderate rent.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke notes the parallel: this is precisely the case in India, where the king has the fifth part of all crops. Keil and Delitzsch trace the terms: a fifth to Pharaoh, four parts to the people for seed, food, and households.

The verse fixes the terms. A fifth of the harvest to Pharaoh, four parts to the people. The rent is light and the people keep the most.

So the witnesses see Joseph set the terms of the new tenancy, and the expositors mark their moderation. The witnesses note the arrangement: “in the increase… ye shall give the fifth part unto Pharaoh, and four parts shall be your own” — of each future harvest, one fifth is to go to the crown as rent, while four fifths remain to the people. They mark the breadth of what the people keep: their four parts serve “for seed of the field, and for your food, and for them of your households, and for food for your little ones” — enough for sowing the next crop, for their own sustenance, and for their families and children; the tenant’s portion is ample. They observe the expositors’ judgement that this rent was light and even generous: a fifth to the sovereign, who had bought the land and saved their lives, while the cultivators retained four fifths of all they raised. They note Clarke’s comparison with India and other lands, where the king has claimed a fifth of all crops as a settled tax; the proportion was a recognized and not oppressive one. They observe that the terms, far from enslaving the people, left them in possession of the bulk of their produce and free to live and prosper as Pharaoh’s tenants. They note that the policy, completed in these terms, secured both the crown’s revenue and the people’s welfare. They observe that the moderation of the rent answers the charge of cruelty. The verse thus fixes the terms: a fifth to Pharaoh, four parts to the people — an arrangement they gratefully receive, in the next verse, as their salvation.
25:
On Genesis 47:25 — “And they said, Thou hast saved our lives: let us find grace in the sight of my lord, and we will be Pharaoh’s servants” — the witnesses mark the people’s gratitude.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: the people were more than satisfied with Joseph’s regulations; if he made them dependent on Pharaoh, he had broken the yoke of the smaller lords. The Reformed Gill: he preserved them from death through famine, and this favour they thankfully owned.

The verse records their thanks. The people own that Joseph saved their lives and gladly serve Pharaoh. Far from feeling wronged, they are grateful.

So the witnesses see the people, so far from resenting the arrangement, receive it with hearty gratitude. The witnesses note their acknowledgment: “Thou hast saved our lives” — they own that Joseph, by his foresight and his administration, has preserved them from the death by famine that must otherwise have overtaken them; Gill marks that they thankfully confessed this great favour. They mark their willing submission: “let us find grace in the sight of my lord, and we will be Pharaoh’s servants” — they accept their new condition gladly, content to be the king’s tenants and servants, asking only Joseph’s continued favour. They observe Ellicott’s point that the people were more than satisfied with Joseph’s regulations; and that if he had made them dependent on the Pharaoh, he had at the same time freed them from the yoke of the many petty lords and hereditary princes who had ruled the districts of Egypt — so that their lot may well have been bettered, not worsened. They note that this grateful response from the people themselves is the strongest answer to the charge that Joseph reduced them cruelly to slavery: those who lived under the policy blessed him for it as their deliverer. They observe that Joseph’s severity in the testing was love, and his administration of the famine, mercy. They note that the saved acknowledge their saviour. The verse thus records their gratitude: thou hast saved our lives — the people willingly Pharaoh’s servants, the law confirmed in the next verse.
26:
On Genesis 47:26 — “And Joseph made it a law over the land of Egypt unto this day, that Pharaoh should have the fifth part; except the land of the priests only… became not Pharaoh’s” — the witnesses mark the lasting statute.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: that the people should hold their land of the king and give him the fifth of the produce as a yearly tax; beyond this the king had no further demand. The Reformed Gill: with the consent of Pharaoh and the people, it became a fundamental law of their constitution, continuing to later times.

The verse records the enduring law. The fifth to Pharaoh became a standing statute, the priests excepted. Joseph’s policy is fixed in lasting law.

So the witnesses see Joseph’s arrangement settled into a permanent law of the land. The witnesses note its establishment: “Joseph made it a law over the land of Egypt unto this day, that Pharaoh should have the fifth part” — what began as an emergency measure became a fixed and lasting statute; Gill observes that it was enacted with the consent of Pharaoh and the people alike, and so became a fundamental law of the Egyptian constitution, still in force in Moses’s day (“unto this day”) and continuing to later times. They mark the moderation the expositors find in the settled terms: as Clarke draws out, the people held their land of the king and rendered him a fifth of the produce as a yearly tax, and beyond this the crown made no further demand; the burden was light and the people secure in their holdings. They observe the standing exception repeated: “except the land of the priests only… became not Pharaoh’s” — the priests’ estate, kept by the king’s allowance, remained free of the tax and the sale. They note that the permanence of the law shows the wisdom and acceptability of Joseph’s policy: it endured for ages and was owned as just. They observe that the chapter thus closes Joseph’s administration of the famine with a settled, equitable order, the crown enriched and the people preserved and content. They note that the law stands as the monument of his statesmanship. The verse thus records the lasting statute: the fifth to Pharaoh, the priests excepted — before the narrative returns, in the next verses, to Israel in Goshen.
27:
On Genesis 47:27 — “And Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt, in the country of Goshen; and they had possessions therein, and grew, and multiplied exceedingly” — the witnesses mark the prospering of Israel.

The grammatical-historical witnesses note the return to Israel. The Puritan Henry: while the Egyptians were impoverished in their own land, Jacob was replenished in a strange land. The Reformed Calvin: they dwelt there, not as proprietors in perpetuity, but as sojourners granted a dwelling-place.

The verse turns back to Israel. Settled in Goshen, they have possessions, grow, and multiply greatly. The family thrives while Egypt is impoverished.

So the witnesses see the narrative return from Egypt’s distress to Israel’s flourishing in Goshen. The witnesses note the settlement: “Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt, in the country of Goshen; and they had possessions therein” — the family is fixed in the region granted them, holding lands and houses there. They mark Calvin’s careful qualification that they dwelt there not as outright owners in perpetuity, as the Egyptians held their portions, but as sojourners to whom Pharaoh had granted a dwelling-place; their true inheritance remained Canaan. They observe the great increase: “and grew, and multiplied exceedingly” — in Goshen the household of Jacob, a company of seventy souls, began to multiply rapidly into the great nation God had promised; the famine that impoverished Egypt became the occasion of Israel’s growth. They note the striking contrast Henry draws: while the Egyptians, in their own land, were impoverished, stripped of money, cattle, and fields, the sojourning Israelites in their corner of that land were replenished, prospered, and multiplied; God blessed the strangers amid the natives’ want. They observe the providence at work: the very removal to Egypt, which might have seemed a calamity, was the means of the family’s preservation and astonishing increase, fulfilling the promise to Abraham. They note that the seed of the covenant flourishes in the place of refuge. The verse thus records the prospering: Israel grows and multiplies exceedingly in Goshen — the setting for Jacob’s last years, which the next verse counts.

So the witnesses see the narrative return from Egypt’s distress to Israel’s flourishing in Goshen. They note the settlement: “Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt, in the country of Goshen; and they had possessions therein” — fixed in the granted region, holding lands and houses. They mark Calvin’s qualification that they dwelt there not as outright owners in perpetuity but as sojourners granted a dwelling-place, their true inheritance still Canaan. They observe the great increase: “grew, and multiplied exceedingly” — the household of seventy beginning to multiply rapidly into the promised nation. They note the striking contrast Henry draws: while the Egyptians were impoverished in their own land, the sojourning Israelites were replenished and prospered; the very removal to Egypt, which might have seemed a calamity, was the means of the family’s preservation and astonishing increase, fulfilling the promise to Abraham.
28:
On Genesis 47:28 — “And Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years: so the whole age of Jacob was an hundred forty and seven years” — the witnesses mark Jacob’s years in Egypt.

The grammatical-historical Wesley notes the symmetry: seventeen years Jacob had nourished Joseph, and now seventeen years Joseph nourished him; he lived far beyond his own expectation. The Reformed Gill: he lived the same term in Egypt as Joseph had with him in Canaan. Calvin: it was no small temptation to the holy old man to be an exile from Canaan so many years.

The verse counts Jacob’s last years. He lived seventeen years in Egypt, dying at a hundred forty-seven. His pilgrimage draws toward its close.

So the witnesses see Jacob’s last years numbered, in a quiet providence full of meaning. The witnesses note the span: “Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years” — the patriarch, who had come down expecting soon to die, was granted seventeen years more, far beyond his own expectation, to see his family established and Joseph’s prosperity. They mark the beautiful symmetry Wesley and others draw out: just as Jacob had nourished and reared Joseph for the first seventeen years of the boy’s life, before he was lost to him, so now Joseph nourished and sustained his father for the seventeen years of his old age in Egypt — Providence ordering a fitting return of a son’s care for a father’s. They observe Gill’s note that Jacob spent the same number of years with Joseph in Egypt as Joseph had spent with him before; the reckoning is exact. They note the total: “the whole age of Jacob was an hundred forty and seven years” — a long pilgrimage, yet (as he had told Pharaoh) short of his fathers’. They observe Calvin’s reflection that it was no small trial to the holy old man to live so long an exile from the promised land, yet his faith held fast its hope in Canaan. They note that these peaceful closing years crown a life of labour and sorrow. The verse thus counts Jacob’s days: seventeen years in Egypt, a hundred forty-seven in all — his death now drawing nigh, as the next verse declares.
29:
On Genesis 47:29 — “And the time drew nigh that Israel must die: and he called his son Joseph… put… thy hand under my thigh, and deal kindly and truly with me; bury me not, I pray thee, in Egypt” — the witnesses mark Jacob’s charge concerning his burial.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “put thy hand under my thigh” — the ancient form of solemn oath (Genesis 24:2). The Reformed Calvin: Jacob’s invincible magnanimity, that no wealth of Egypt could allure him from sighing for Canaan. Wesley: Israel, who had power over the angel, yet must yield to death; his candle gradually burnt down. The Puritan-minded note his faith in the promise.

The verse opens Jacob’s last charge. Feeling death near, he binds Joseph by oath not to bury him in Egypt. His heart is fixed on Canaan.

So the witnesses see the dying Jacob summon Joseph and bind him by a solemn oath concerning his burial. The witnesses note the approach of the end: “the time drew nigh that Israel must die” — the patriarch, who had wrestled with the angel and prevailed, must yet, like all men, yield to death; Wesley observes that his candle was not blown out but gradually burned down, so that he saw the time drawing near. They mark the solemn form of the charge: “put… thy hand under my thigh” — the ancient gesture of a binding oath, the same Abraham had used with his servant (Genesis 24:2); Jacob would have Joseph swear. They observe the substance: “deal kindly and truly with me; bury me not, I pray thee, in Egypt” — he asks the last kindness, that his body not be left in the land of his sojourning. They note the faith and magnanimity Calvin finds here: no wealth or honour of Egypt, where his son ruled, could allure Jacob to be content to rest there; his heart sighed still for the land of Canaan, the land of promise. They observe that this charge is an act of faith in God’s covenant: by insisting on burial in Canaan, Jacob testifies that the promised land, not Egypt, is the true inheritance of his seed. They note that even in dying he looks beyond Egypt to the promise. The verse thus opens his last charge: bury me not in Egypt — the request he completes, in the next verse, by naming his fathers’ grave.
30:
On Genesis 47:30 — “But I will lie with my fathers, and thou shalt carry me out of Egypt, and bury me in their buryingplace. And he said, I will do as thou hast said” — the witnesses mark the request to rest with his fathers.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: God had promised Canaan to Abraham’s posterity, and Jacob considered it a consecrated place; Sarah, Abraham, and Isaac were buried near Hebron. The Reformed Gill: he desired it partly to express his faith in the promised land, partly to draw off his posterity’s hearts from Egypt. Calvin: “I will sleep” — the word “sleep” for death refers to the body, not the soul.

The verse completes the charge. Jacob asks to be carried to Canaan and buried with his fathers; Joseph consents. His burial confesses the promise.

So the witnesses see Jacob name the place of his desired rest, and Joseph give his word. The witnesses note the request: “I will lie with my fathers, and thou shalt carry me out of Egypt, and bury me in their buryingplace” — Jacob asks to be carried back to Canaan and laid in the family grave, the cave of Machpelah near Hebron, where Abraham, Sarah, and Isaac already lay. They mark the meaning the expositors find in this insistence. Gill discerns a twofold purpose: partly Jacob would express his own faith in the promised land, owning Canaan as the destined inheritance of his seed; and partly he would draw the hearts of his posterity away from Egypt and fix them on Canaan, lest, settling comfortably there, they forget the promise. They observe Clarke’s point that, since God had promised Canaan to Abraham’s posterity, Jacob regarded the land, and the burial-place of the patriarchs in it, as a consecrated spot under God’s special blessing. They note Calvin’s remark on the word “sleep”: that to “lie” or “sleep” with the fathers speaks of the body laid in the grave, not of the soul, which lives; the figure of sleep for death applies to the body. They observe Joseph’s ready compliance: “I will do as thou hast said” — the son promises to fulfil the father’s wish. They note that the burial in Canaan is a standing testimony to the covenant. The verse thus completes the charge: carry me to my fathers’ grave — the promise Joseph gives and Jacob, in the next verse, has confirmed by oath.
31:
On Genesis 47:31 — “And he said, Swear unto me. And he sware unto him. And Israel bowed himself upon the bed’s head” — the witnesses mark the oath and Jacob’s worship.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the closing scene. Gill: Jacob required the oath, not from distrust of Joseph, but to show how intensely his mind was set on this. Adam Clarke: Jacob, old and feeble, reclined on his couch. Ellicott: the LXX and Hebrews 11:21 read “upon the top of his staff” — the Hebrew, without vowels, may mean either bed or staff.

The verse closes the chapter. Joseph swears; Jacob bows in worship. The patriarch’s last act is reverence toward God.

So the witnesses see Jacob secure the oath and bow in worship, closing the chapter on an act of faith. The witnesses note his insistence: “Swear unto me. And he sware unto him” — Jacob will have Joseph bind the promise with an oath. They mark Gill’s point that this was not from any distrust of his son, whom he knew faithful, but to show how intensely his whole mind was set on this matter, and to make the obligation the more sure and solemn, that nothing might prevent his being buried in Canaan. They observe the closing gesture: “Israel bowed himself upon the bed’s head” — the aged patriarch, content that his last wish was secured, bows in reverence. They note the well-known textual variation the expositors record: the Hebrew, written without vowels, may be read either “bed” or “staff,” and the Septuagint, followed by the Epistle to the Hebrews (Hebrews 11:21), reads “he worshipped, leaning upon the top of his staff”; both readings agree in the act of worship. They observe that whether he bowed at the head of his couch or leaned on his staff, the meaning is the same: Jacob, with his weak and failing body, exerts himself to give thanks and to worship God, who had granted his desire and kept his covenant. They note that the patriarch’s last recorded act is one of faith and adoration, fixed on the promise. The verse thus closes the chapter: Joseph sworn, and Israel worshipping — the dying patriarch’s reverent trust in God.
